content:
# 1 John Chapter 2 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1JN_001_page_001.jpg, 1JN_002_page_001.jpg

1. My little children, I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin. But if anyone does sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous.

2. He is the propitiation for our sins, and not for ours only but also for the sins of the whole world.

3. And by this we know that we have come to know him, if we keep his commandments.

4. Whoever says "I know him" but does not keep his commandments is a liar, and the truth is not in him,

5. but whoever keeps his word, in him truly the love of God is perfected. By this we may know that we are in him:

6. whoever says he abides in him ought to walk in the same way in which he walked.

7. Beloved, I am writing you no new commandment, but an old commandment that you had from the beginning. The old commandment is the word that you have heard.

8. At the same time, it is a new commandment that I am writing to you, which is true in him and in you, because the darkness is passing away and the true light is already shining.

9. Whoever says he is in the light and hates his brother is still in darkness.

10. Whoever loves his brother abides in the light, and in him there is no cause for stumbling.

11. But whoever hates his brother is in the darkness and walks in the darkness, and does not know where he is going, because the darkness has blinded his eyes.

12. I am writing to you, little children, because your sins are forgiven for his name's sake.

13. I am writing to you, fathers, because you know him who is from the beginning. I am writing to you, young men, because you have overcome the evil one.

14. I write to you, children, because you know the Father. I write to you, fathers, because you know him who is from the beginning. I write to you, young men, because you are strong, and the word of God abides in you, and you have overcome the evil one.

15. Do not love the world or the things in the world. If anyone loves the world, the love of the Father is not in him.

16. For all that is in the world — the desires of the flesh and the desires of the eyes and pride in possessions — is not from the Father but is from the world.

17. And the world is passing away along with its desires, but whoever does the will of God abides forever.

18. Children, it is the last hour, and as you have heard that antichrist is coming, so now many antichrists have come. Therefore we know that it is the last hour.

19. They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would have continued with us. But they went out, that it might become plain that they all are not of us.

20. But you have been anointed by the Holy One, and you all have knowledge.

21. I write to you, not because you do not know the truth, but because you know it, and because no lie is of the truth.

22. Who is the liar but he who denies that Jesus is the Christ? This is the antichrist, he who denies the Father and the Son.

23. No one who denies the Son has the Father. Whoever confesses the Son has the Father also.

24. Let what you heard from the beginning abide in you. If what you heard from the beginning abides in you, then you too will abide in the Son and in the Father.

25. And this is the promise that he made to us — eternal life.

26. I write these things to you about those who are trying to deceive you.

27. But the anointing that you received from him abides in you, and you have no need that anyone should teach you. But as his anointing teaches you about everything, and is true, and is no lie — just as it has taught you, abide in him.

28. And now, little children, abide in him, so that when he appears we may have confidence and not shrink from him in shame at his coming.

29. If you know that he is righteous, you may be sure that everyone who practices righteousness has been born of him.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

Chapter 2 is the letter's ethical core. The "test of knowing" (vv.3-6), the love commandment (vv.7-11), the three addresses (vv.12-14), the world warning (vv.15-17), and the antichrist section (vv.18-27) together establish the criteria for genuine Christian community. The key move: claims must be tested by conduct, and the supreme test is love of brother.

**Verse 2 — "Propitiation":** The most theologically loaded word in the chapter. PNEUMA renders it "the sacrifice that atones" — a reasonable dynamic equivalent but one that removes the specific connotation of hilasmos (turning away wrath / removing sin's barrier).

**Verse 20 — "You all know":** Sinaiticus reads pantes ("all") not panta ("all things") — emphasizing the community's universal access to truth through the anointing.

---
## ⚠️ Disagreement Log

### v.2 — "propitiation" vs. "the sacrifice that atones"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the propitiation for our sins" | hilasmos peri tōn hamartiōn hēmōn |
| LOGOS | "the propitiation for our sins" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the sacrifice that atones for our sins" | Dynamic equivalent for hilasmos |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. hilasmos is a technical theological term that means "the means of propitiation/expiation." "Sacrifice that atones" captures expiation (removing sin) but not propitiation (averting wrath). The term is debated in scholarship precisely because it carries both senses. Retaining "propitiation" preserves the theological density and the scholarly conversation around it.

**Status:** ⚠️ Theologically significant. "Propitiation" vs. "atonement sacrifice" affects whether divine wrath is in view.

---

### v.7 — "the old commandment is the word that you have heard" vs. "this old commandment — to love one another — is the same message you heard before"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The old commandment is the word which you heard" | hē entolē hē palaia estin ho logos hon ēkousate |
| LOGOS | "The old commandment is the word that you have heard" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "This old commandment — to love one another — is the same message you heard before" | Adds "to love one another" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author does not specify the content of the old commandment in v.7 — he says it is "the word that you have heard." While the love commandment is clearly in view (cf. John 13:34), the author withholds the specification until the reader connects the dots. PNEUMA inserts "to love one another" as an explanatory gloss, removing the reader's work.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA specifies what the author leaves implicit.

---

### v.16 — "pride in possessions" vs. "pride in our achievements and possessions"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the boastfulness of life" | hē alazoneia tou biou |
| LOGOS | "pride in possessions" | Same sense, natural English |
| PNEUMA | "pride in our achievements and possessions" | Adds "achievements" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The Greek alazoneia tou biou means "the boastfulness of life/livelihood" — bios refers to the resources and circumstances of life. PNEUMA adds "achievements," which is not in the text. The author's triad is precise: desire of flesh, desire of eyes, boast of livelihood. Adding "achievements" expands the third category beyond what bios covers.

**Status:** Minor. Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

### v.20 — "anointing" vs. "the Holy Spirit"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you have an anointing from the Holy One" | chrisma echete apo tou hagiou |
| LOGOS | "you have been anointed by the Holy One" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the Holy One has given you his Spirit" | "Spirit" for chrisma (anointing) |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. The author says chrisma ("anointing"), not pneuma ("spirit"). While the anointing likely refers to the Holy Spirit, the author's metaphor is deliberate — chrisma connects to Christos (the Anointed One), creating a wordplay: you have an anointing (chrisma) from Christ (Christos). PNEUMA replaces the metaphor with the referent, losing the wordplay.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. PNEUMA replaces metaphor with referent.

---

### v.27 — "the anointing teaches you" vs. "the Spirit teaches you"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "his anointing teaches you concerning all things" | to chrisma autou didaskei hymas peri pantōn |
| LOGOS | "his anointing teaches you about everything" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the Spirit teaches you everything you need to know" | "Spirit" for "anointing"; adds "you need to know" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS. Same issue as v.20 — PNEUMA replaces chrisma with "Spirit" and adds "you need to know" (not in the text). The author says the anointing teaches "about everything" (peri pantōn), not "everything you need to know." The qualifier limits the scope.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Consistent with v.20 decision.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Description |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA replaces metaphor with referent | 2 | "Spirit" for "anointing" (vv.20, 27) |
| PNEUMA specifies the implicit | 1 | "To love one another" added to old commandment (v.7) |
| PNEUMA adds to text | 2 | "Achievements" (v.16), "you need to know" (v.27) |
| PNEUMA changes theological terms | 1 | "Sacrifice that atones" for "propitiation" (v.2) |

**Overall pattern:** Chapter 2's most significant disagreement is v.2 — hilasmos. Whether Christ is a "propitiation" (averting wrath and removing sin) or a "sacrifice that atones" (removing sin only) affects the theology of the cross. The anointing/Spirit swap (vv.20, 27) is also notable: PNEUMA consistently interprets the metaphor rather than translating it, losing the chrisma/Christos wordplay.

---

*— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA*
